text: Tomatoes are among the most important food and economic vegetable crops worldwide because of their extensive cultivation, their many forms of consumption, and their use in numerous food industries. They are consumed not only fresh, but are also used to produce tomato paste, juices, sauces, ketchup, peeled and canned tomatoes, powders, concentrates, and other products. Tomatoes occupy a prominent position within agricultural systems because they are a relatively high-value crop with rapid economic turnover and intensive labor and input requirements. They are also linked to a broad chain of activities that includes seed and seedling production, fertilizers and pesticides, crop operations, harvesting, transport, grading, packaging, marketing, and food processing. However, this major economic importance makes the tomato crop highly sensitive to any decline in yield or quality. Exposure to a serious insect pest does not merely mean losing some leaves or fruits; it can also reduce marketable yield, increase control costs, cause some consignments to be rejected, raise the proportion of losses, and disrupt supply schedules for factories and markets. Among the pests that have radically transformed tomato production systems worldwide in recent decades, Tuta absoluta—known as the tomato leaf miner or tomato borer—stands out as one of the most dangerous invasive pests threatening tomato production in both open fields and protected cultivation.

2.1 Global Production Volume Tomatoes are grown in most agricultural regions worldwide, from temperate and Mediterranean areas to tropical and subtropical zones. According to statistics from the Food and Agriculture Organization, global tomato production reached approximately 192 million tonnes in 2023, ranking tomatoes first among vegetable crops in terms of worldwide production volume that year. [1] This enormous production volume reflects the importance of tomatoes to global food security and shows how sensitive markets are to major disruptions in key production areas, whether caused by pests, diseases, heat waves, water shortages, or supply-chain disturbances. The importance of tomatoes is not limited to countries that produce them for domestic consumption. Some countries depend on tomatoes as: A crop for the fresh export market. A major processing crop. A source of agricultural and industrial employment. An important component of food security. A raw material for tomato paste and sauce factories. A strategic crop in regional and international trade. 2.2 Nutritional Value Tomato fruits contain a range of important nutritional and health-related compounds, including: Vitamin C. Potassium. Organic acids. Carotenoids. Lycopene. A range of phenolic compounds and antioxidants. Lycopene is one of the most important compounds associated with the red color of tomato fruits and is also a major quality indicator in tomato cultivars intended for processing. Its value and concentration are affected by the cultivar, ripening stage, temperature, nutrition, and the health status of the plant and fruit. Tomato quality is not limited to nutritional value. It also includes color, firmness, size, shape, soluble-solids content, acidity, and freedom from insect and disease damage. 2.3 Processing Importance The production objectives for processing tomatoes differ from those for tomatoes intended for fresh consumption. Processing plants place greater emphasis on characteristics such as: High soluble-solids and Brix levels. A strong, uniform red color. High lycopene content. A low proportion of defects. Fruit firmness and ability to withstand transport. Ease of harvesting. A high proportion of fruit ripening at the same time. Low losses during grading and processing. Insect infestation directly affects these characteristics. An infested fruit may be rejected even when its size and color are suitable because the presence of mines, holes, or larvae inside the fruit makes it commercially and industrially unacceptable. Insect damage also opens the way for secondary rot pathogens, increasing the proportion of damaged fruit, raising grading costs, and reducing the quantity of raw material suitable for processing. 2.4 Social and Economic Importance The value of the tomato crop extends beyond the price of the fruit sold, as it supports large numbers of workers in: Nurseries. Seed production. Crop husbandry. Irrigation and fertigation networks. Pest control. Harvesting. Packaging. Transport. Wholesale and retail markets. Food-processing plants. Quality and pesticide-residue analysis laboratories. Consequently, any major crisis in tomato production transmits its effects from the field to the other links in the value chain. Farmers’ income may decline, some employment may stop, factory operating efficiency may fall, consumer prices may rise, or the need to import processed products may increase.

3.1 The Position of Tomatoes in Egyptian Agriculture Tomatoes are among Egypt’s most important vegetable crops in terms of area, production, and consumption. Climatic conditions and the diversity of production areas allow them to be grown in multiple seasons and planting windows, keeping the crop available in markets for long periods of the year. Tomato cultivation is distributed across many areas, including: Delta governorates. The Nile Valley. Old agricultural lands. Nubaria. Wadi El Natrun. The northwestern coast. Areas west of Minya. Beni Suef and Fayoum. Upper Egypt. New and reclaimed lands. An earlier Food and Agriculture Organization assessment of Egypt’s tomato value chain reported national production of about 7.9 million tonnes in 2016, placing Egypt fifth among tomato-producing countries at that time. This figure is cited here as a historical indicator of the scale of the sector, not as a fixed figure for every season. [2] 3.2 Multiple Growing Seasons and Production Areas Egypt’s multiple tomato-growing seasons help maintain supplies to markets and processing plants, but they also create a major pest-management challenge. When tomato fields of different ages are located close together, pests can move from older fields to newer plantings. Abandoned fields, crop residues, volunteer plants, or wild solanaceous hosts may become continuous sources of pest reproduction and spread. This phenomenon is sometimes known as the Green Bridge, in which suitable host plants remain available to the pest for long periods, preventing its life cycle from being completely interrupted between seasons. The problem becomes more evident when a grower stops controlling a heavily infested field before the plants are removed, shredded, and ploughed under; large numbers of adult moths then migrate into neighboring, younger fields. 3.3 Fresh-Market and Processing Tomatoes Tomatoes are produced in Egypt for two main purposes: Production for the fresh market This focuses on shape, size, color, firmness, tolerance to handling, and freedom from visible fruit defects. Production for processing This focuses on Brix, color, concentration, firmness, uniform ripening, low defect levels, and efficient conversion into paste or concentrates. In both systems, Tuta absoluta infestation poses a direct threat. In the fresh market, holes, mines, and deformities lower the market grade of the fruit. In processing tomatoes, infestation increases grading and rejection rates, raises losses, and may cause loads exceeding acceptable defect and infestation limits to be rejected. 3.4 The Impact of Losses on the Value Chain Tomatoes are naturally relatively perishable and are strongly affected by poor handling, transport delays, high temperatures, and mechanical and insect damage. Crop loss therefore includes not only fruit that falls in the field, but also: Infested fruit that cannot be sold. Fruit rejected at grading stations. Fruit rejected by the processing plant. Losses during transport. Fruit rotted as a result of insect wounds. Price reductions caused by poor quality. Loss of the added value that processing could have generated. The Food and Agriculture Organization has conducted specific studies on losses in Egypt’s tomato value chain, reflecting the economic importance of reducing losses from the field through to the consumer. [2]

sectionTitle: 4. Why Are Pests an Economic Threat?
text: 4.1 The Difference Between Pest Presence and Economic Damage The presence of one insect or a limited number of insects does not necessarily mean that an economic loss requiring chemical intervention has occurred. An economic decision should be linked to pest density, the life stage present, crop age, the proportion of infested plants or fruit, crop value, intervention cost, and the damage expected if no control action is taken. Pest management distinguishes among three main concepts: Infestation The presence of the pest or its life stages on the plant or within its tissues. Damage The pest’s actual effect on plant tissues, such as mining, feeding, or fruit injury. Economic Loss The financial reduction caused by crop loss, lower quality, or higher production costs. A plant may tolerate a limited infestation, while another small infestation occurring at a sensitive time—such as the beginning of fruit set or during fruit filling—may cause a much greater loss. 4.2 Direct Yield Loss Pests feed on different parts of the plant and may cause: Seedling death. Reduced leaf area. Weakened photosynthesis. Damage to growing points. Flower desiccation. Fruit-set drop. Fruit infestation. Fruit rot. Reduced total yield. Reduced marketable yield. With Tuta absoluta, damage is not confined to the leaf. Larvae can attack leaves, stems, growing points, and fruit, extending their impact across vegetative growth, yield, and quality. 4.3 Decline in Market Quality A field may produce a high total weight, yet a large proportion may be unsaleable because of infestation. This point is extremely important because the true economic measure is not always total yield, but rather: Marketable yield or yield accepted for processing. Fruit with holes, larvae, or rot may be included when estimating total field weight, but it does not represent actual income for the grower. In fresh tomato markets, a small hole in the fruit can sharply reduce its commercial value. In processing plants, infested fruit may increase defects and grading losses and may also increase the microbial load, affecting the safety and quality of the raw material. 4.4 Increased Production Costs Pests add multiple items to the cost of producing a feddan, including: Pesticide costs. Spraying and operating costs. Labor costs. Purchase of traps and pheromones. Monitoring and inspection costs. Cost of biological-control agents. More frequent field visits. Cost of removing heavily infested plants. Crop-residue disposal costs. Residue-analysis costs. Cost of repeat spraying when a treatment fails. When spraying is random, a grower may incur high costs without achieving real pest control, especially when the pesticide is applied at the wrong time or against a life stage concealed within plant tissue. 4.5 Pesticide Residues and Pre-Harvest Intervals Excessive or poorly regulated pesticide use may lead to: Exceeding maximum residue limits. Failure to comply with the pre-harvest interval. Rejection of domestic or export consignments. Difficulty marketing the crop to processors that follow specifications. Higher residue-testing costs. Restrictions on the use of certain active ingredients. Damage to the reputation of the farm or supplying company. This issue is especially sensitive in tomatoes because fresh-market cultivars are harvested repeatedly, while processing tomatoes may be approaching a single, large-scale harvest. 4.6 Development of Insecticide Resistance Resistance develops when individuals that are more tolerant of an insecticide survive and reproduce, causing resistance traits to become more common in the pest population over successive generations. The risk of resistance increases when: The same active ingredient is repeatedly used. Products with different trade names but the same mode of action are used. Rates below the recommendation are applied. Sprays are repeated without monitoring. Treatments provide incomplete coverage. Control relies entirely on chemicals. The pest produces many generations during the season. Studies have shown that Tuta absoluta can develop resistance to different insecticide groups through changes in target-site sensitivity or an increased ability to detoxify the insecticide. [3] 4.7 Killing Natural Enemies Broad-spectrum pesticides may eliminate parasitoids and predators naturally present in the field. When natural-enemy densities decline, the agricultural environment loses part of its natural ability to regulate pest populations. In some cases, pest numbers decline briefly after spraying and then rise rapidly again because of: Survival of some resistant individuals. Death of natural enemies. Migration of new moths from neighboring fields. Continued presence of eggs and larvae inside mines. Failure to remove infestation sources from old fields. The success of a spray should therefore be evaluated not only by the number of insects killed immediately, but by a sustained decline in the pest population and the preservation of biological balance within the field. 4.8 Economic-Loss Equation The economic impact of a pest can be expressed in simplified form by the following relationship: Total economic impact of the pest = Loss of marketable yield Price reduction due to lower quality Control costs Grading and rejection costs Supply or export losses Consequences of insecticide resistance Indirect environmental and health damage. This shows that the most dangerous pest is not necessarily the one that consumes the greatest amount of plant tissue, but the one that combines rapid reproduction, difficult detection, high control costs, attack on the marketable part of the plant, and the ability to develop resistance.

sectionTitle: 5. Introduction to the Threat of Tuta absoluta
text: 5.1 What Is Tuta absoluta? Tuta absoluta is a small lepidopteran insect native to South America. Within a relatively short period, it became a widespread invasive pest threatening tomato cultivation in Europe, Africa, Asia, and the Middle East. It is known worldwide by names such as: Tomato leaf miner. Tomato borer. South American tomato moth. South American tomato pinworm. The name tomato leaf miner is widely used, but it does not describe every form of damage because the larvae attack not only leaves, but also stems, growing points, flowers, and fruit. The larval stage is responsible for direct damage. The larva mines into plant tissue and feeds in a location that is relatively protected from external conditions and from many surface spray treatments. 5.2 Why Is It One of the Most Dangerous Tomato Pests? The threat posed by Tuta absoluta results from the combination of several biological and behavioral characteristics in a single pest. First: Concealed Feeding For much of its active period, the larva lives within leaf, stem, or fruit tissue. Spray droplets may therefore reach the plant surface without reaching the concealed larva at an effective concentration. The presence of larvae inside mines also makes early infestation more difficult to detect unless plants are inspected regularly and carefully. Second: High Reproductive Capacity The insect can produce successive generations throughout the year when suitable temperatures and host plants are available. Its relatively short life cycle in warm conditions can cause numbers to rise rapidly if an infestation is not detected early. Third: Attacking Different Crop Stages Infestation can begin at the seedling stage and continue through vegetative growth, flowering, fruit set, fruit filling, and up to the period close to harvest. The economic impact varies according to the timing of infestation: Early infestation may weaken growth and crop establishment. Damage to growing points may deform growth. Leaf infestation reduces effective leaf area. Fruit infestation causes a direct loss in the marketable portion of the crop. Fourth: Attacking Multiple Parts of the Plant Larvae can attack: Leaves. Petioles. Growing points. Tender stems. Calyces. Green fruit. Fruit that has begun to change color. Ripe fruit. This variety of infestation sites makes control more difficult and means that monitoring based on traps alone is insufficient. Fifth: High Potential Losses Monitoring protocols and plant-protection reports indicate that severe, unmanaged infestations can cause losses ranging from 80 to 100% under some conditions, in both protected cultivation and open fields. [4] This figure must be interpreted carefully. It represents the potential limit during severe outbreaks in the absence of effective control and does not mean that every infested field will automatically lose this proportion of its crop. Actual loss depends on: When the infestation begins. Pest density. Plant age. Cultivar. Condition of the crop canopy. Temperature. Presence of nearby infestation sources. Monitoring efficiency. Speed of intervention. The type of control program used. Sixth: Rapid Spread Movement of fruit, seedlings, packaging materials, and agricultural trade, together with the insect’s ability to disperse locally, helped Tuta absoluta move from its native range into extensive new areas. After it was recorded in Spain in 2006, it spread rapidly across Mediterranean countries, Africa, and Asia and became a major threat to tomato production in most areas where it established. [5] Seventh: Ability to Develop Resistance Tuta absoluta has a short life cycle and high reproductive capacity, two factors that increase the selection of resistant individuals when insecticides with the same mode of action are repeatedly applied. Resistance to multiple insecticide groups has been recorded in different pest populations, making rotation among IRAC groups, reduction of chemical selection pressure, and integration of non-chemical controls essential rather than optional. [3] Eighth: Movement Between Fields Adult moths may move from old or neglected fields, or from fields where growers have stopped control measures, into neighboring newer fields. The risk of this migration increases when: Infested plants are left after harvest ends. Shredding and ploughing are delayed. Growing seasons overlap. Alternative hosts or volunteer plants are widespread. Traps are not used in production areas. Each grower works independently without coordinating with neighboring growers. Management of Tuta absoluta therefore cannot be limited to the boundaries of a single field; it requires area-wide management across the agricultural zone and the production community.

Egyptian conditions provide several factors that may help the pest persist, including warm weather during much of the year, multiple tomato-growing seasons, overlapping plantings, the presence of potato, eggplant, and other solanaceous hosts, and the retention of crop residues in some areas. Movement of the pest from one growing season to another may maintain pest pressure throughout the season. Each field should therefore be viewed as part of a wider production system that includes: The nursery. Neighboring fields. Fields where harvesting has ended. Solanaceous crops. Weeds and wild hosts. Packing stations. Containers used to transport fruit and seedlings. Grading and processing waste. In open-field processing tomatoes, infestation may appear early and then increase as the canopy grows and plants overlap. If the canopy deteriorates at the end of the season, fruit becomes more exposed to heat and secondary damage while larvae continue attacking fruit close to harvest. Fields that mature late may also be exposed to moth migration from earlier fields where harvesting has ended or control programs have stopped.

sectionTitle: 7. Why Is Chemical Control Alone Insufficient?
text: Global experience has shown that reliance on insecticides alone does not provide sustainable control of Tuta absoluta. The insect is protected inside mines during part of its life, and eggs, larvae, pupae, and adult moths may all be present in the field at the same time. Repeated spraying may also lead to: Selection of resistant strains. Death of natural enemies. Increased pesticide residues. Higher production costs. Failure of some active ingredients. Fewer available options close to harvest. Disruption of biological balance in the field. Modern scientific reviews therefore emphasize that effective management of Tuta absoluta must be based on Integrated Pest Management (IPM), combining monitoring, traps, crop practices, biological control, and selective insecticides within a single program. [5, 6] Applying IPM does not mean completely avoiding insecticides. It means using an insecticide when needed, at the correct time, with an appropriate active ingredient, and after assessing pest density, while preserving natural enemies as far as possible and preventing resistance development.

sectionTitle: 8. Initial Principles for Successful Pest Management
text: Before turning to the practical details in the following chapters, the general foundations of successful control can be summarized as follows: Prevent the pest from entering with infested seedlings. Install pheromone traps early, before severe infestation appears. Inspect plants in the field and do not rely on traps alone. Remove heavily infested leaves or plants at the beginning of an outbreak when feasible. Dispose of crop residues promptly after harvest. Prevent neglected fields from remaining a source of moths. Control weeds and alternative hosts. Protect naturally occurring natural enemies. Use biological and microbial control agents within a clear program. Select insecticides according to the active ingredient and mode of action, not merely the trade name. Rotate insecticide groups according to the IRAC classification. Comply with recommended rates and pre-harvest intervals. Evaluate results by infestation levels and damaged fruit, not by the number of sprays. Coordinate control among neighboring fields whenever possible.

Tuta absoluta is not merely an insect that attacks tomato leaves. It is a complex economic pest affecting yield, quality, control costs, crop safety, and the sustainability of insecticide programs. Its threat arises from its ability to: Remain concealed within plant tissues. Produce multiple generations. Attack leaves, stems, and fruit. Move rapidly between fields and regions. Survive on multiple plant hosts. Develop insecticide resistance. Cause severe losses when detection is delayed. Successful management therefore begins before economic damage appears, through prevention, monitoring, and early detection, followed by an integrated set of cultural, biological, behavioral, and chemical measures. References

Tuta absoluta is one of the most dangerous insect pests attacking tomato crops in both open fields and protected cultivation. It has earned this status because it can infest multiple plant parts, reproduces rapidly, conceals its larvae within plant tissues, moves easily between fields as adult moths, and can develop resistance to several insecticides. The danger of Tuta absoluta is not measured solely by the number of insects in the field, but by its ability to cause direct damage inside leaves, stems, growing points, and fruit. At the start of an infestation, a plant may look acceptable externally even though larvae have already begun forming mines within its tissues. Understanding the insect, the life stage responsible for damage, and the infestation process is therefore essential before establishing any monitoring or control program.

Tuta absoluta is a small moth in the order Lepidoptera, which includes butterflies and moths. It belongs to the small-moth family Gelechiidae, whose many species have larvae that bore or feed within plant tissues. The commonly used scientific name is: Tuta absoluta (Meyrick) Some recent taxonomic references also use the name: Phthorimaea absoluta (Meyrick) The use of a different genus name in some sources does not mean that two different pests are involved. It reflects taxonomic revisions of relationships among genera in this group. Tuta absoluta remains the name most widely used in agricultural guides, control programs, pheromone traps, and agricultural trade. The insect undergoes complete metamorphosis and passes through four main stages: Egg. Larva. Pupa. Adult moth. Each stage has a different role in the life cycle. The egg begins a generation; the larva feeds, grows, and causes damage; the pupa is the transformation stage; and adult moths mate, disperse, and lay eggs. Tomato is the pest’s principal economic host, although it can infest other plants, particularly species in the nightshade family.

sectionTitle: 2.3 Is Tuta absoluta a Moth, a Leafminer, or a Worm?
text: These names describe different aspects of the insect: Moth: the winged adult insect. Larva or worm: the stage that hatches from the egg and feeds on the plant. Leafminer: a description of how the larva feeds within plant tissue. Tomato leafminer: a common name for the pest, although it does not describe all forms of damage. Tomato borer or tomato fruit borer: names referring to its ability to penetrate fruit and stems. Weevil: a colloquial name used by some growers, but taxonomically incorrect because the insect is not a true weevil in the order Coleoptera. Tuta absoluta is therefore not a “worm” throughout its life. It is a moth whose worm-like larval stage bores into the plant and causes damage.

Infestation usually begins when a female lays eggs on plant parts. A small larva then hatches, searches for a suitable entry point, and quickly penetrates a leaf, stem, bud, or fruit. Once inside plant tissue, the larva begins feeding and forms a mine or mined patch. The appearance of damage varies with the infested plant part and the age of the larva. Tuta absoluta is a pest that: Mines leaves. Bores into stems and petioles. Attacks growing points. Bores into fruit. Causes both direct and indirect damage. Can infest plants from the nursery stage until close to harvest. Describing it only as a “leafminer” is therefore incomplete, because the most serious economic losses are often associated with damage to growing points, stems, and fruit.

sectionTitle: 2.5 Nature of the Damage Caused by the Pest
text: Damage caused by Tuta absoluta can be divided into five interrelated types: Direct feeding damage. Physiological damage. Structural damage. Indirect damage caused by secondary infection. Economic and market damage. 2.5.1 Direct Feeding Damage Direct damage occurs when larvae feed on plant tissues. In leaves, a larva consumes the mesophyll between the upper and lower epidermis, while the two outer epidermal layers may remain temporarily intact. This produces pale or translucent areas known as: Mines or mined patches As feeding continues and the mine expands, the damaged area gradually turns brown, dries out, and dies. In stems, petioles, and growing points, larvae feed within tissues and form tunnels that may interfere with the movement of water and nutrients and with the continued growth of young plant parts. In fruit, the larva enters beneath the skin or into the fleshy tissue and forms tunnels usually accompanied by feeding debris and frass. 2.5.2 Physiological Damage Leaf infestation does more than create dry patches; it reduces the effective leaf area available for photosynthesis. When infestation is severe, it may cause: Reduced sugar production within the plant. Poor fruit nutrition. Reduced vegetative growth. A lower capacity for fruit filling. Reduced final yield. Fruit exposure to sunlight after canopy collapse. A greater risk of sunscald and elevated fruit temperature. A lower capacity to tolerate heat and water stress. The severity of these effects depends on the percentage and location of infested leaves, plant age, timing of damage, and the canopy’s ability to continue producing new leaves. Not every leaf mine represents the same loss. Damage to an old lower leaf is not equivalent to damage affecting several young leaves or the growing point during fruit set and filling. 2.5.3 Structural Damage to Stems and Growing Points Larvae may bore into: Leaf petioles. Young growth. Buds. Growing points. Tender stems. The area where the calyx joins the fruit. Damage to the growing point of young plants has more serious consequences than the loss of part of a leaf because it may cause: Cessation of main-stem growth. Death of the growing point. Irregular lateral growth. Delayed plant development. An imbalance between vegetative and reproductive growth. Poor formation of early clusters. Loss of part of the early yield. Tunnels within the stem may also weaken tissues and cause the portion beyond the damaged area to wilt, especially when several larvae are present or when infestation overlaps with plant pathogens. 2.5.4 Direct Damage to Fruit Fruit infestation is the most economically serious form of damage because the pest directly reaches the part harvested and sold or delivered to a processing plant. Larvae may enter fruit: Near the calyx and pedicel. Where fruit touches leaves or other fruit. From the sides of the fruit. Through cracks or weak areas in the skin. Sometimes from the lower side or other externally inconspicuous areas. Infested fruit may show signs such as: Small entry holes. Exit holes. Dark frass near an opening. Shallow tunnels beneath the skin. Soft or sunken areas. Discoloration around the tunnel. A larva or cast skin inside the fruit. Rot developing around the damaged site. The entry hole may be extremely small compared with the damage inside the fruit. A quick external inspection is therefore insufficient, particularly when evaluating crops intended for processing or export. 2.5.5 Indirect Damage and Secondary Infection The mines, holes, and wounds made by larvae provide entry points for pathogens, especially rot-causing fungi and bacteria. A limited insect infestation can therefore develop into compound damage involving: A wound caused by larval entry. Tunnels within the tissue. Moisture and frass accumulating in the tunnel. Entry of pathogenic microorganisms. Tissue breakdown and decay. Complete loss of the fruit. Rot may continue to develop during harvest, transport, and temporary storage, so losses may continue after the crop has left the field. This problem intensifies under warm, humid conditions, when fruit is piled, transport is delayed, or additional mechanical wounds are present. 2.5.6 Economic and Market Damage Infested fruit does not have to fall or disappear for an economic loss to be recorded. It may remain on the plant and count toward total yield while being unmarketable. Forms of economic loss include: Rejection of infested fruit in the fresh market. Downgrading. A higher cull rate. Higher labor costs. Rejection of entire loads when infestation is high. Greater processing losses. A higher microbial load in raw material. Less sound fruit available for processing. More sprays and higher control costs. Difficulty complying with pre-harvest intervals. A higher risk of pesticide residues. Lower profits for growers and processors. Fields should therefore be assessed by marketable yield rather than total fruit weight alone.

2.6.1 The Larva Is Responsible for Damage The larval stage is the damaging stage in the life cycle of Tuta absoluta. The larva passes through several instars and consumes more tissue as it matures. Newly hatched larvae begin with small mines, which expand as they grow. A larva may move from one mine to another or from a leaf to another plant part. All larval instars can cause damage, although the nature of that damage differs among them: Newly Hatched Larvae They are characterized by being: Extremely small. Quick to search for an entry point. Able to start tiny mines that are difficult to detect. Potentially more vulnerable to control during the short interval between hatching and tissue entry. Important targets for biopesticides and materials acting by contact or ingestion. Intermediate and Advanced Larval Instars They are characterized by: Consuming more tissue. Forming wider mines. Producing more visible frass. Being able to attack stems and fruit. Causing most of the visible damage. Receiving greater protection within plant tissue. Sometimes leaving a mine and moving to a new site. Large larvae and extensive mines should not be allowed to appear before action is taken, because by then a substantial amount of damage has already occurred. 2.6.2 Why Is the Egg Not Considered a Damaging Stage? The egg does not feed on the plant and therefore causes no direct damage. It is, however, an important early indicator of a new generation before damage begins. Monitoring eggs is useful because killing or parasitizing them before hatching prevents larvae from emerging. This is why egg parasitoids such as Trichogramma species and inspection of young leaves before mines appear are important. The egg is not a damaging stage, but it is a critical stage for prevention. 2.6.3 Why Is the Pupa Not Considered a Damaging Stage? The pupa is a non-feeding stage in which the larva transforms into an adult moth. It therefore causes no direct loss of plant tissue. The presence of pupae in soil, leaves, or residues means that a new generation of moths will soon emerge. Residue management, tillage, and disposal of infested plants are therefore important for reducing pest sources. 2.6.4 Why Is the Adult Moth Not Directly Damaging? Adult moths, if they feed at all, consume liquids; they neither bore into tomato tissue nor make the mines found in leaves and fruit. The moth does not directly cause visible damage, but it has major epidemiological importance because it is responsible for: Mating. Egg laying. Movement between plants. Migration between fields. Reinfestation of young plantings. Spread of the pest over new distances. The moth can therefore be described as the dispersal and reproductive stage, while the larva is the damaging stage. This distinction is essential when interpreting pheromone traps: a trap measures adult male activity and does not directly count larvae within plants.

sectionTitle: 2.7 How Damage Develops, Step by Step
text: The infestation sequence can be simplified as follows: Stage One: Moths Reach the Field Moths may arrive from: A neighboring infested field. Residues from an old crop. Infested seedlings. Other solanaceous hosts. Volunteer plants or host weeds. Contaminated crates or transport equipment in some cases. Stage Two: Egg Laying Females lay eggs on suitable plant parts, especially young tissues and leaves, and may also lay them near stems, calyces, and fruit. Stage Three: Hatching A very small larva emerges from the egg and begins searching for an entry point. Stage Four: Tissue Entry The larva bores into a leaf, stem, or fruit and becomes less exposed to external influences after entry. Stage Five: Mine Expansion The larva continues feeding and growing, the damaged area expands, and frass accumulates within the mine or around openings. Stage Six: Movement A larva may leave a mine and move to another leaf, stem, or fruit, so an empty mine does not mean that the infestation has ended. Stage Seven: Pupation and Emergence of a New Generation After completing its growth, the larva pupates. A new moth then emerges, lays eggs, and repeats the infestation cycle. Overlapping generations result in eggs, larvae, pupae, and moths being present in the field at the same time. This complicates control and prevents reliance on a single treatment.

sectionTitle: 2.8 Why Is It Difficult for Insecticides to Reach the Larva?
text: After penetrating plant tissue, the larva becomes partly enclosed by layers of leaf, stem, or fruit. Spray solution may therefore cover the outside of the plant without reaching the larva at an effective dose. Control becomes more difficult when: Larvae are older. Larvae are deep inside fruit. The canopy is dense. Spray coverage is poor. Water volume is insufficient. Spray does not reach both leaf surfaces. The product is unsuitable for the target stage. Spraying is delayed until mines have expanded. Resistance to the active ingredient is present. Timing is therefore crucial. Intervention should preferably target the period immediately before or after hatching, before most larvae become established within tissues. This timing cannot be determined from traps alone. Trap data must be combined with inspections for eggs, recent mines, larvae, and infested plants.

2.9.1 A Successful Invasive Pest Tuta absoluta has several traits that helped it change from a local South American pest into a widely distributed international pest, including: Rapid reproduction. Multiple generations. Small size. Ease of concealment. Ability to move with plant products. Ability to fly and disperse locally. Availability of alternative hosts. Adaptation to a wide range of environmental conditions. Rapid colonization of new production areas. The pest creates particular pressure where tomato crops are continuous or overlap, leaving no adequate period without a plant host. 2.9.2 Infestation at Every Crop Stage Infestation may begin in the nursery and continue until the end of harvest. The form of loss therefore varies by crop stage: Nursery Weak seedlings and transfer of the pest to the field After transplanting Slow establishment and damage to growing points Vegetative growth Reduced leaf area Flowering and fruit set Disrupted growth and damage to young tissues Fruit filling Reduced photosynthesis and fruit infestation Color development and harvest Fruit rejection, greater losses, and pre-harvest-interval constraints This long period makes pest management a continuous program, not a remedial spray applied after damage appears. 2.9.3 Overlapping Generations Under suitable conditions, the field may contain all of the following at the same time: Newly laid eggs. Small larvae. Large larvae. Pupae. Adult moths. A treatment may therefore affect one stage while the others remain and rebuild the population shortly afterward. Differences in crop age and multiple planting dates in the area also allow the pest to keep moving from one field to another. 2.9.4 Capacity to Cause Severe Losses In severe outbreaks that are not detected early or managed effectively, a very large proportion of the crop may be damaged, and marketable-yield losses may approach complete loss in extreme cases. The estimate of “100% losses” should not be applied generally to every infestation. Actual losses vary according to several factors, including: Infestation start date. Egg and larval density. Percentage of infested fruit. Crop age. Cultivar. Temperature. Monitoring quality. Speed of intervention. Condition of neighboring fields. Natural-enemy levels. Effectiveness of the control program. Early pest presence does not make crop loss inevitable, but it does require a rapid, monitoring-based response. 2.9.5 Ability to Develop Resistance Tuta absoluta populations in many regions face intense selection pressure because insecticides are used repeatedly. With multiple generations and large populations, individuals capable of tolerating a particular active ingredient are more likely to survive. These individuals then reproduce, reducing the pest population’s susceptibility to the insecticide. Resistance increases when: The same active ingredient is repeated. The same IRAC group is repeated. Different trade names from the same group are used. Rates are reduced without technical justification. Spraying occurs at the wrong time. Control relies on insecticides alone. Migration continues from fields with different spray programs. A large number of treatments is applied during the season. Insecticide resistance is one of the main reasons Tuta absoluta remains difficult to control and persistently dangerous. 2.9.6 Movement from Old Fields to New Plantings A field where harvest has ended can become a major source of moths if infested plants are left without shredding or ploughing. A dangerous scenario develops when: Infestation rises in an old field. Harvest nears its end. The grower stops spraying. Infested plants remain in the ground. New moths emerge in large numbers. They move into younger fields. Infestation begins in the new plantings. Crop-residue management is therefore not cosmetic; it directly breaks the pest cycle and protects neighboring fields. 2.9.7 Low Acceptance Threshold for Infested Fruit A plant may tolerate limited leaf infestation, but infested fruit is usually commercially unacceptable. A single hole may lead to: Culling the fruit. Downgrading it. Entry of rot organisms. Rejection during grading. A higher defect rate in the load. The economic threshold for fruit infestation is therefore much lower than the threshold associated with a limited number of leaf mines. In processing tomatoes, a distinction must be made among: Total yield. Red-ripe yield. Sound yield. Yield accepted by the factory. The proportion of infested, rotten, or rejected fruit.

sectionTitle: 2.10 Difference Between Active and Old Infestation
text: Seeing a mine on a leaf does not necessarily mean a live larva is present at inspection. The larva may have: Remained inside the mine. Left and moved to another leaf. Died. Been parasitized. Completed its growth and left to pupate. Several mines should therefore be opened and examined to determine whether the infestation is active. Signs of Active Infestation A live larva. Fresh frass. Recently chewed tissue. Continued mine expansion. Small, recent mines. New eggs close to growing areas. Signs of Old Infestation A completely dry mine. Dead brown tissue. Absence of the larva. An exit hole. No further mine expansion. Confusing old and active infestation leads to inaccurate spray decisions. Insecticides may be used because old damage is present even though it contains no live larvae, or a field may be neglected because moth catches are low despite active larvae within the plants.

sectionTitle: 2.11 Indicators to Monitor When Assessing Risk
text: Tuta absoluta should not be assessed using a single indicator. A better assessment combines: Number of moths in traps. Week-to-week trend in trap catches. Percentage of plants bearing eggs. Percentage of plants with recent mines. Number of live larvae. Percentage of infested growing points. Percentage of fruit with holes. Distribution of infestation within the field. Infestation intensity at field edges compared with the interior. Presence of neighboring old or neglected fields. Presence of natural enemies or parasitized eggs. Crop age and time remaining until harvest. Risk increases when rising trap catches coincide with new eggs, recent mines, live larvae, and infested fruit.

Misconception One: Tuta absoluta Attacks Leaves Only Incorrect. It can infest leaves, petioles, stems, growing points, and fruit. Misconception Two: The Moth Makes Holes in Fruit Incorrect. The larva bores into fruit, while the moth lays eggs and spreads the infestation. Misconception Three: If No Moths Are Seen, the Field Is Pest-Free Incorrect. Moths are small and may be more active at specific times, while larvae remain concealed within the plant. Misconception Four: High Trap Catch Equals the Same Percentage of Damage Not accurate. A trap measures male activity, while damage depends on eggs, larvae, their location, and crop age. Misconception Five: Every Mine Requires an Immediate Spray Not accurate. The mine must be checked to determine whether it is recent and contains a live larva, and the spread of infestation and other field indicators must be assessed. Misconception Six: Changing the Trade Name Prevents Resistance Incorrect. Several trade names may contain the same active ingredient or belong to the same IRAC group and mode of action. Misconception Seven: More Sprays Mean Better Control Incorrect. Indiscriminate sprays may increase resistance, residues, and costs and kill natural enemies without achieving sustainable control.

Tuta absoluta is an invasive moth whose larval stage causes direct damage. Larvae begin feeding within leaves and can also attack growing points, stems, and fruit, producing overlapping physiological, market, and economic damage. Eggs and pupae cause no feeding damage, while the adult moth is the reproductive and dispersal stage. Distinguishing among these stages is essential for selecting and timing control measures. The pest’s overall danger arises from a combination of factors: Larval concealment within tissues. Infestation of multiple plant parts. Overlapping generations. Continued infestation from nursery to harvest. Movement between fields. Susceptibility of infested fruit to rot and rejection. High potential for insecticide resistance. Difficulty of relying on a single control method. Detecting the pest only after mines have spread and fruit has been infested is therefore relatively late. Real success begins by preventing entry with seedlings, monitoring moths and eggs, detecting young larvae before they establish within tissues, and applying an integrated program of cultural, biological, behavioral, and chemical measures. References

The pest Tuta absoluta is known by a number of scientific, international, Arab and local names. This diversity is due to the insect's transmission between several genera during its taxonomic history, its spread in different geographical and linguistic regions, and the diversity of plant parts that its larvae attack. Some names focused on the tunnels that the larvae make inside tomato leaves, while other names focused on their ability to mine stems, growing tips, and fruits. Some names are also linked to the insect's original habitat in South America. In Egypt, the pest is known primarily as the “tuta,” and some farmers also call it the “weevil” or “tomato weevil.” It is sometimes confused with a pest known in the field as the leaf miner, although the latter name often refers to tunneling flies of the genus Liriomyza, not Tuta absoluta. This is why understanding the names of the pest is linked to the correct diagnosis. Because Tuta absoluta and leafminer flies differ in classification, shape, life cycle, damage sites, and monitoring and control programs.

The modern scientific taxonomic name of the insect is: Phthorimaea absoluta Meyrick, 1917 The name by which the pest has become known internationally in recent decades is: Tuta absoluta (Meyrick, 1917) The two names refer to the same insect species, not two different pests. Tuta absoluta has been the name most often used in scientific papers, extension publications, integrated management programs, pheromone traps, and biocontrol products. Then a recent taxonomic study returned the insect to the genus Phthorimaea, and its modern taxonomic name became Phthorimaea absoluta. [1, 2] This is why the insect appears in references and databases in two forms: Phthorimaea absoluta. Tuta absoluta. The name “Tuta absoluta” remains the most famous name among engineers, farmers and workers in tomato production.

The scientific name consists of two parts: Genus name Phthorimaea It begins with a capital letter when written in Latin letters. Type name absoluta It starts with a lowercase letter. The genus and species name are written in italics: Phthorimaea absoluta The name of the scientist who described the species and the year of description are not written in italics: Phthorimaea absoluta Meyrick, 1917 The scientist's name appears in parentheses when the previous name is written: Tuta absoluta (Meyrick, 1917) Brackets mean that the insect was not first described within the genus Tuta, but was transferred to it after its original description. After writing the full name for the first time, it is shortened to: P. absoluta Or, in references that use the common name: T. absoluta

Edward Meyrick British entomologist Edward Merrick described the insect in 1917 based on specimens collected from Peru in South America. When it was first described, it bore the name: Phthorimaea absoluta Meyrick, 1917 It was then reclassified several times as a result of changing understanding of the relationships between the genera of the tribe Gnorimoschemini within the family Gelechiidae. Historically, the insect appeared under the following names: Phthorimaea absoluta. Gnorimoschema absoluta. Scrobipalpula absoluta. Scrobipalpuloides absoluta. Tuta absoluta. Phthorimaea absoluta again. The name Tuta absoluta became widespread after the insect was reassigned to the genus Tuta in 1994. A taxonomic and morphological analysis published in 2021 indicated that the species is in a group that includes Phthorimaea operculella, which is the typical species of the genus Phthorimaea., and accordingly the insect was returned to its original genus. [1, 2]

All of the following names refer to the same pest: Scientific name Taxonomic status Phthorimaea absoluta Meyrick, 1917 Modern taxonomic name Tuta absoluta (Meyrick, 1917) The most famous and used name Gnorimoschema absoluta Previous scientific name Scrobipalpula absoluta Previous scientific name Scrobipalpuloides absoluta Previous scientific name These names do not mean the existence of different species, but rather represent the stages through which the species has been classified. The importance of the previous names becomes apparent when reviewing old studies. Because some research on the biology, spread and control of the pest was published before the name Tuta absoluta. became widespread.

In the European and Mediterranean Organization for Plant Protection (EMPO) database, the insect bears the symbol: EPPO Code: GNORAB The first part of this code goes back to the previous name: Gnorimoschema absoluta The symbol remained constant despite the insect's transfer between different genera. This code allows records of the same species to be linked within international databases, regardless of the change in the scientific name. The pest appears in plant protection databases under: Phthorimaea absoluta. Tuta absoluta. GNORAB. [1]

3.7.1 Tomato leafminer The literal translation of this name is: Tomato leafminer The name Tomato leafminer is used in a large number of international references and publications on Tuta absoluta. The name describes the ability of the larvae to enter between the two layers of a leaf and feed on the inner tissue, resulting in burrowing spots or tunnels. But Leafminer is not a taxonomic name specific to a single species, but rather a general description of any insect whose larvae feed within leaf tissue. Therefore, there are different types of insects named Leafminer, and they belong to different orders and families. 3.7.2 South American tomato leafminer Meaning: South American tomato borer Or: Tomato leafminer in South America The name links: The pest is native to South America. Behavior of the burrowing caterpillar inside tomato leaves. The descriptor “South American” reduces confusion with other leafminer species. 3.7.3 Tomato leafminer moth Meaning: Tomato burrowing moth The name combines the appearance of the adult insect with the harmful nature of the larvae. The adult is a small moth, while the larva is the one that enters plant tissues and makes tunnels. 3.7.4 South American tomato moth Meaning: South American tomato moth He points out that the insect: A small moth of the order Lepidoptera. Originated in South America. Economically related to tomato yield. This name is considered more accurate than the word “butterfly” when describing the entire insect, because the insect belongs to the group of small moths. 3.7.5 Tomato borer Meaning: Tomato borer This name reflects the fact that Tuta absoluta's damage is not limited to the leaves. Larvae can attack: Papers. Leaf petioles. Developing peaks. Legs. The cup. The fruits. The name Tomato borer describes burrowing behavior in plants more broadly than the name Tomato leafminer. 3.7.6 Tomato pinworm The name Tomato pinworm appears in some references associated with Tuta absoluta, but this name is also used mainly for another pest: Keiferia lycopersicella Both pests belong to the Gelechiidae species, and their larvae cause damage to tomato leaves and fruits, but they are two different species. [4] Therefore, the name Tomato pinworm alone does not identify the pest without mentioning the scientific name. 3.7.7 South American tomato pinworm Meaning: South American tomato pinworm The phrase South American is added to differentiate Tuta absoluta from other pests bearing the name Tomato pinworm. However, it remains a name: Tuta absoluta. South American tomato moth. More clearly when talking about this scourge.

sectionTitle: 3.8 Common names in a number of languages
text: The pest has spread to large areas of the world, and has different local names. Language Common name Approximate meaning English Tomato leafminer Tomato leafminer English South American tomato moth South American tomato moth English Tomato borer Tomato borer French Mineuse de la tomate Tomato borer Spanish Polilla del tomate Tomato moth Spanish Minador del tomate Tomato borer Spanish Gusano minador del tomate Tomato burrowing caterpillar Spanish Cogollero del tomate Growth apex borer in tomatoes Italian Tignola del pomodoro Tomato moth Portuguese Traça-do-tomateiro Tomato leaf moth German Tomatenminiermotte Tomato burrowing moth Dutch Tomatenmineermot Tomato burrowing moth Turkish Domates güvesi Tomato moth These names revolve around three main elements: Tomato crop as the primary economic breadwinner. Burrowing into tissue as a larval behavior. The moth as a complete insect form.

The pest appears in Arabic references under several names, including: Tuta absoluta. Tomato leaf and fruit miner. Miner of tomato leaves and fruits. Tomato borer. Tomato borer. South American tomato moth. South American tomato moth. Tomato leafminer. The name is: Tuta absoluta — borer of tomato leaves and fruits One of the names that most expresses the identity of the pest and the nature of its damage. The word “Tuta absoluta” specifies the type of insect, while the description “tomato leaf and fruit miner” makes it clear that the damage is not limited to the leaf only. 3.9.1 Tuta absoluta The name “Tuta absoluta” represents the transliteration of the common scientific name: Tuta absoluta This name is distinguished by the fact that it identifies the pest directly, and does not confuse it with other types of burrowing insects. It is widely used in: Scientific research. Agricultural extension bulletins. Field visit reports. Control programs. Fisheries records. Pheromone traps. Biological control programmes. Pesticide and seed companies. 3.9.2 Tomato leaf and fruit borer This name expresses the most important sites of damage caused by the larvae. They often begin by feeding inside the leaves, and can then attack: Developing peaks. Leaf petioles. Legs. Flowers. The cup. Green and ripe fruits. This name reflects the economic severity of the pest because infestation of fruits leads to a direct loss of the marketable or industrial crop. 3.9.3 Tomato leaf miner This name represents the literal translation of the name: Tomato leafminer It describes the tunnels or burrowing spots that caterpillars make inside leaves. But the name may cause confusion in Egyptian field use because the expression "leaf miner" or "leaf miner" is often used to refer to tunneling flies of the genus Liriomyza. Also, describing Tuta absoluta as a leaf miner only does not include its infestation of stems, tops, and fruits. 3.9.4 Tomato borer This name describes the behavior of the larvae inside tomato plants in general, but it does not alone determine the type of pest because tomatoes are exposed to several insects that may burrow into the leaves, stems, or fruits. The specific name is always associated with Tuta absoluta: Tomato borer Tuta absoluta. 3.9.5 South American tomato moth This name describes the entire insect and its native habitat. The adult insect is a small moth from the family Gelechiidae, while its original habitat is South America before it spread to Europe, Africa, Asia and the Middle East.

sectionTitle: 3.10 Names of the pest in Egypt
text: 3.10.1 Tuta “Tuta” is the most common name among farmers and agricultural engineers in Egypt. It is an abbreviation of the name: Tuta absoluta Expressions such as: Injury to the Tuta. Tuta butterfly. Tuta larvae. Tuta eggs. Tuta trap. Tuta pheromone. Count Tuta adults. Tuta control programme. The name “tuta” refers to the pest in all its stages, while the function of each stage differs: The egg represents the beginning of the generation. The larva causes damage. Virgo is in the process of transformation. The adult moth is responsible for mating, dispersing, and laying eggs. 3.10.2 Tuta butterfly The expression “Tuta butterfly” is used to refer to the winged adult. The most accurate expression from the insect perspective is: Tuta moth Because the adult insect is small in size and belongs to the moth group. The adult moth does not burrow into the leaves or fruits, but rather mates and lays eggs. Direct damage occurs after the eggs hatch and the larvae emerge. 3.10.3 Tuta worm Farmers use the term “Tuta worm” to refer to the stage found inside the leaves or fruits. The scientific name for this phase is: Tuta larvae It is the phase responsible for: Formation of tunnels in leaves. Mine growing tips and stems. Fruit penetration. Creating holes and droppings. Opening entrances for secondary molds. 3.10.4 Tomato borer The name “tomato borer” is used in Egyptian extension and technical reports, and describes the method of feeding larvae inside plant tissues. These larvae differ from the larvae that feed exposed on the surface of the leaf because they spend a large part of their feeding period protected inside: The paper. Leg. Top. The fruit. 3.10.5 Weevil or tomato weevil Some farmers call it: The weevil. Tomato weevil. Fruit weevil. On Tuta absoluta, especially when holes or larvae are seen inside the fruits. This name is a popular name that does not express the true classification of the pest. Fatuta absoluta belongs to: Order Lepidoptera — Lepidoptera It is the order that includes moths and butterflies. The true weevils belong to: Order Coleoptera — Coleoptera It is the order that includes beetles and weevils. Adjective Tuta absoluta Real weevils Rank Lepidoptera Coleoptera The whole insect Small moth Beetle The damage-causing phase Butterfly larva Varies depending on type Front wings Membranous covered with scales Often stiff Field name Tuta The weevil Thus, Tuta absoluta is not a true weevil, even if it is given that name in some agricultural areas.

sectionTitle: 3.11 The difference between tomato and leaf miner in tomatoes
text: 3.11.1 Meaning of the word Leaf miner The word means: Leaf miner Literally: Leaf leafminer or leaf miner. It is not a scientific name for a single insect species, but rather a description of how the larvae feed within the leaf tissue. The burrowing insect may be: Fly. Moth. Beetle. Insect of another order. Therefore, the presence of a tunnel inside the leaf alone is not sufficient to determine the type of insect. 3.11.2 What is meant by fiber miner in the field in Egypt? When the name “leaf miner” is used in Egyptian tomato fields, what is often meant is: Leafminer flies — Liriomyza spp. The well-known species within this genus are: Liriomyza trifolii. Liriomyza sativae. Liriomyza bryoniae. These insects belong to: Order Diptera Diptera. Agromyzidae. species The adult is a small fly, often black and yellow, while the larvae has no visible legs and resembles a small white or yellow worm. Liriomyza larvae make thin, winding or serpentine tunnels inside the leaves. [3] 3.11.3 Tuta absoluta is not a leaf miner fly Tuta absoluta belongs to: Order Lepidoptera Lepidoptera. Gelechiidae. species The adult insect is a small moth, not a fly. As for the larvae, it is a moth larva that has: Clear head. Thoracic legs. Ventral legs. A body that changes color with age. The larvae often form wide, irregular tunnels or spots inside the leaf, and there may be dark droppings inside the tunnel. It can also move from leaves to stems, tops, and fruits. [5] 3.11.4 Comparison between Tuta and Leafminer Comparison Tuta absoluta Fiber Miner Liriomyza Scientific name Phthorimaea absoluta Liriomyza spp. Field name Tuta Leafminers or leafminers Rank Lepidoptera Diptera The whole insect Small moth Little fly Caterpillar form A butterfly larva with a head and legs The larva of a legless fly Tunnel shape Macular or wide, often irregular Thin, sinuous or often serpentine Excrement It collects inside parts of the tunnel They appear as lines or points within the path Affected parts Leaves, stems, tops and fruits Basically papers Injury to fruits Popular and economically important It does not represent the usual damage Pheromone trap Used for Tuta monitoring No Tuta pheromone is used for this pest Complete stage Moth Fly 3.11.5 Shape of the Tuta tunnels Tuta infestation sometimes begins as a narrow tunnel, then widens as the larvae grows to become: Transparent spot. Wide tunnel. Irregular white or gray area. A spot containing dark droppings. Dry, brown tissue in an old infestation. Several tunnels may merge, destroying a large area of the leaf. The larvae can exit a tunnel and move to another leaf, stem, or fruit, so empty tunnels may be found alongside tunnels containing live larvae. 3.11.6 Liriomyza tunneling format Liriomyza Fly tunnels start out as thin lines, then gradually become wider as the larvae grows. They are often characterized by: Zigzag. Snake. Relatively long. confined within the paper. It contains a trail of waste inside the tunnel. Some tunnels may widen or overlap as the injury is severe, but they often retain the characteristic winding appearance. [3]

sectionTitle: 3.12 The difference between Tuta and other similar pests
text: 3.12.1 Tuta and tomato fruitworm Tomato fruitworm is often associated with the species: Helicoverpa armigera It is a larger larvae than the Tuta larvae, feeds more openly, and makes relatively large holes in the fruits. The Tuta larvae are smaller, and usually begin inside the leaves, then enter the fruit through a small opening that may be found near the calyx or in other places on the surface of the fruit. 3.12.2 Tuta and potato tuber moth Potato tuber moths are: Phthorimaea operculella As for Tuta absoluta, it is: Phthorimaea absoluta They are two different species within the same genus. The potato tuber moth is mainly associated with potatoes and their tubers, while tomatoes are the main economic host of Tuta absoluta. There is a relative similarity between the two adult insects, so accurate identification requires examination of diagnostic characteristics or the use of specialized pheromone traps for each species. 3.12.3 Tuta and tomato pinworm In North America, tomato pinworms are known as: Keiferia lycopersicella Its larvae can form spot tunnels in the leaves and then attack the fruits. This insect differs from Tuta absoluta, although they belong to the Gelechiidae family and there is a similarity in the type of damage. [4]

term What is meant? Tuta Tuta absoluta in all its stages Tuta moth Full winged insect Tuta larvae The burrowing stage that causes the damage Tuta eggs Eggs laid by females on plants Tuta pupa Metamorphosis occurs before the moth emerges Tuta Tunnel A spot or fossilized area within a leaf Infestation of Tuta fruits Larval holes or tunnels inside fruits fiber miner Liriomyza spp. Leafminer Flies The weevil An inaccurate popular name for the tutu Tuta pheromone A sex pheromone that attracts Tuta males Tuta trap A trap containing a pest-specific pheromone

sectionTitle: 3.14 The importance of the correct name in diagnosis and control
text: Accurate nomenclature is not only a linguistic issue, but is directly related to the integrated management programme. Confusing Tuta with leafminer flies may lead to: The use of a pheromone is not related to the existing pest. Misinterpretation of trap results. Improper pesticide selection or timing. Confusing a butterfly larva with a fly larva. Neglecting to inspect the fruits and stems. Recording inaccurate monitoring data. Applying a control program that targets a different insect. Incorrect assessment of the effectiveness of vital agents. When there are tunnels inside tomato leaves, diagnosis includes examining: Tunnel shape. The presence of a larvae inside it. The shape of the caterpillar's head and legs. There is an injury to the tops or stems. The presence of holes in the fruits. Complete insect form. Results of pheromone traps. Mine shape alone does not identify the pest in every case, especially early in an infestation or when several mines overlap.

The modern taxonomic scientific name for Tuta absoluta is: Phthorimaea absoluta Meyrick, 1917 The most famous name internationally and in the field is: Tuta absoluta (Meyrick, 1917) Its international names include: Tomato leafminer. South American tomato leafminer. South American tomato moth. Tomato leafminer moth. Tomato borer. South American tomato pinworm. Its Arabic names include: Tuta absoluta. Tomato leaf and fruit miner. Tomato borer. South American tomato moth. Tomato leafminer. In Egypt, it is mainly known as: Tuta. Tuta butterfly. Tuta worm or larvae. Tomato borer. Weevil, a popular name that is taxonomically incorrect. Tuta absoluta does not represent the “leaf miner” in the common field sense in Egypt. Leafminer often refers to tunneling flies of the genus Liriomyza, which are small flies of the order Diptera. As for Tuta absoluta, it is a moth of the order Lepidoptera, and its larvae cause spot tunnels in the leaves, and also burrow into the tops, stems, and fruits. Thus, the basic field distinction is: Tuta: A moth that burrows its larvae into leaves, tops, stems, and fruits. Leaf Miner: A fly whose larvae make winding tunnels primarily inside leaves. Scientific classification is the system that biologists use to arrange organisms into hierarchical groups, starting with broad groups that include large numbers of organisms, then gradually narrowing down to genus and species. Classification is not limited to giving a name to the insect, but rather clarifies its location among living organisms and its degree of relationship to other species. It also helps to understand their anatomical characteristics, life cycle, feeding behavior, and methods of diagnosing and combating them. Tuta absoluta belongs to the arthropods, then to the insects, then to the order Lepidoptera, which includes moths and butterflies. It is placed within the family Gelechiidae, a group that includes small moths, many of whose larvae feed inside plant tissue. The species is currently classified under the name: Phthorimaea absoluta Meyrick, 1917 While the previous name remains: Tuta absoluta (Meyrick, 1917) It is the most famous name in the agricultural field and applied research.
